params.text:
When to escalate

Knowing what to report, and how quickly, keeps people safe. Some things need urgent action — get help immediately. Others should be reported promptly so the nurse can review the person. When in doubt, always escalate; it is never wrong to raise a concern about someone in your care.

  • Urgent: suspected sepsis, no urine draining, catheter out, heavy bleeding.
  • Prompt: signs of infection, bypassing, cloudy urine, discomfort.
  • Always: record what you saw and who you told.

Bringing it together

You have seen how a urinary catheter works, why people have one, and how good hand hygiene, aseptic principles, a closed system and correct bag positioning all help prevent infection. You have also seen how everyday support — careful emptying, gentle hygiene, encouraging fluids, securing the catheter and protecting dignity — keeps people comfortable, and how recognising and reporting problems early keeps them safe.

Finally, remember the boundary this course has stressed throughout. This training is awareness only and does not authorise anyone to carry out catheter care without task-specific training, assessed competence and delegation by a registered nurse. You must not insert or remove a catheter, and you may only carry out delegated catheter care when it is set out in the care plan. Anything abnormal, you escalate to the nurse.
